Q1: My study routine was honestly chaotic but somehow functional. I'd usually carve out time in the evenings after work, putting on lecture recordings while I cooked dinner or did chores around my small apartment. With 20 hours a week to dedicate, I had to be strategic, so I'd batch my studying into longer sessions on weekends when I could really focus. The mindfulness exercises I started doing helped me center myself before diving into material, but some weeks everything just fell apart when life got overwhelming.
Q2: My pattern varied wildly depending on what was due and what crisis was happening in my life that week. I tried to keep Sundays as my main study day, but deadlines would force me into late-night cramming sessions throughout the week. Being 27 and juggling work with limited resources meant I couldn't always stick to an ideal schedule. When assignments were due, I'd sometimes study in concentrated bursts, ignoring everything else until it was submitted.
Q3: Deadlines were my primary motivator for logging in, honestly. I'd get that anxious feeling in my stomach when I knew something was due, and that would push me to check the LMS. Sometimes I'd log in just to download materials so I could study offline later, since my internet at home isn't always reliable. The anxiety of potentially missing something important kept me checking in frequently, even when I wasn't actually engaging deeply with the content.
Q4: After a few weeks, logging in became almost automatic, especially first thing in the morning to check announcements. I'd made it part of my routine to check the LMS during my lunch break at work, just scanning for updates. The mindfulness intervention actually helped me be more intentional about this—I'd do a breathing exercise, then log in with purpose rather than anxious scrolling. It became a coping mechanism in a way, having that structure when everything else felt uncertain.
Q5: The video lectures were absolutely essential for me as an auditory learner. I could listen to them multiple times, sometimes at 1.5x speed when reviewing, and the information stuck so much better than reading. I watched over four hours of video content because hearing explanations really helped concepts click for me. The peer interactions were also valuable—those 33 exchanges with classmates helped me feel less isolated and clarified things I was confused about.
Q6: I barely touched the discussion forums, posting only once the entire course. I felt too anxious to put my thoughts out there publicly, worried I'd say something wrong and embarrass myself. Reading materials I often skimmed or downloaded to read later but never got back to. My low resources meant I couldn't always print things out, and reading long documents on a small phone screen just wasn't effective for how I learn.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could immediately see how it connected to assignments or exams. If I could listen to it while doing something else, even better—that's why the videos were gold for me. Anything that helped reduce my anxiety about upcoming deadlines felt valuable. I also appreciated activities that didn't require perfect internet, since my connection is spotty and nothing's more frustrating than a video buffering every thirty seconds.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford, honestly. With my anxiety already through the roof about required work, adding optional tasks seemed like inviting more stress. Required activities had clear stakes—I knew exactly what would happen if I didn't do them. Optional ones felt like they were designed for students with more time and fewer worries, not someone like me who's barely keeping her head above water.
Q9: Absolutely, most of my actual studying happened offline. I'd download lecture recordings and listen to them on my commute or while doing housework. I filled notebooks with handwritten notes because writing helps me process information, even though I learn best by hearing. I also used free YouTube videos to supplement concepts I didn't understand, and sometimes I'd find better explanations there than in the course materials.
Q10: There were many evenings where I'd be reviewing my handwritten notes for hours, talking through concepts out loud to myself, and none of that showed up anywhere. I'd sometimes record myself explaining topics and play it back to see if I actually understood. Study sessions with my neighbor who's also a student happened at her kitchen table, not online. My LMS activity would show nothing during these times, but I was deeply engaged with the material.
Q11: All the time—I'd download videos and PDFs in one session, then spend days working through them offline. My internet is unreliable enough that I learned early to grab everything I needed when I had a good connection. I'd watch a lecture once online, download it, then review it three or four more times over the following week. The LMS would only show that single initial access, missing all that repeated engagement.
Q12: There was a period mid-semester when my anxiety peaked and I could barely function. I was dealing with some personal stuff—financial stress, uncertainty about my future—and logging into the course felt overwhelming. Even though my resilience is generally high, there were days when just opening my laptop triggered a wave of sadness. The mindfulness exercises helped me push through, but those weeks were really dark and my engagement definitely suffered.
Q13: Fatigue was a constant companion, especially after long work days when I'd try to study in the evenings. When I was exhausted, I'd log into the LMS and just stare at the screen without absorbing anything. Competing responsibilities—work, household stuff, trying to maintain some semblance of a social life—meant the LMS often got whatever scraps of attention I had left. Confusion about course material sometimes made me avoid logging in altogether because I didn't want to face how lost I felt.
Q14: More times than I'd like to admit. I'd have a video playing but my mind would be somewhere else entirely, worrying about bills or replaying some awkward interaction from work. Sometimes I'd scroll through course materials while feeling completely numb, going through the motions without any real learning happening. The sadness I've been carrying made it hard to be present even when I was technically 'engaged' with the content.
Q15: I avoided the discussion forums because putting my thoughts in writing for everyone to see terrified me. I'd postpone reading assignments because my anxiety would spike when I saw how long they were. Sometimes I'd avoid logging in for days because I knew there would be something stressful waiting for me. Even knowing these things were important, my brain would convince me that avoiding them temporarily would help, even though it always made things worse.
Q16: Deadlines basically controlled my entire existence in this course. I'd have periods of minimal activity followed by frantic bursts of engagement as due dates approached. My 96.8% assignment completion rate happened because deadline panic is an incredibly effective motivator for me. I'd set multiple alarms and reminders because my anxious brain was terrified of missing something. The structure of deadlines actually helped contain my anxiety somewhat—at least I knew exactly when things were due.
Q17: Feedback was complicated for me. Good feedback gave me a temporary boost, but I'd quickly start worrying about maintaining that level. Critical feedback, even when constructive, would send me into an anxiety spiral that made it hard to engage for days. I tried to use the mindfulness techniques to process feedback more calmly, with mixed success. Honestly, I sometimes avoided checking my grades because I wasn't sure I could handle the emotional impact.
Q18: The course structure with its clear deadlines and organized modules helped me manage my anxiety somewhat. Having 12 online courses to juggle meant I needed that external structure desperately. The flexibility of being able to watch lectures on my own schedule was essential given my work commitments. However, the structure also felt relentless sometimes—there was always something due, always another module to complete, never a moment to just breathe.
Q19: The video-heavy content made engagement easier for me as an auditory learner—I could actually absorb information that way. The ability to download materials helped enormously given my unreliable internet. What made things harder was the expectation of discussion participation when I was already so anxious about public contribution. The pacing sometimes felt impossible, like the course assumed we had nothing else going on in our lives.
Q20: I think that could reflect someone like me—logging in frequently out of anxiety, checking and rechecking things, watching videos multiple times without necessarily producing visible output. High activity might also mean someone is struggling to understand material and keeps revisiting it without that translating to submitted work. Or they could be dealing with personal issues that make it hard to complete things even when they're trying. The activity shows effort and intention, even if the results don't follow.
Q21: That actually reflects my experience somewhat. My attendance was only 7.5%, which looks terrible, but I still passed and completed almost all my assignments. I did most of my learning offline through downloaded materials and personal study methods that don't show up in logs. Someone could be deeply engaged with course content through textbooks, notes, or external resources and appear disengaged online. The LMS isn't the only place learning happens.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show patterns—when someone logs in, what they click on, how long videos play. They can identify students who might be struggling based on declining activity. But they completely miss the emotional experience of learning, the offline study that happens, the quality of engagement versus just time spent. They can't capture whether someone was crying while writing an assignment or feeling triumphant after finally understanding a concept. The numbers tell a story, but it's never the whole story.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just completing requirements. It's when I'm listening to a lecture and suddenly something clicks and I feel excited about learning, despite all my anxiety and sadness. Engagement is showing up even when it's hard, even when my mental health is struggling, because I believe this education matters for my future. It's the internal commitment to learning that persists even when external metrics might not reflect it.
Q24: The LMS data wouldn't show the tears I cried during particularly stressful weeks, or the pride I felt when I finally understood difficult concepts. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent with my handwritten notes, talking through material out loud, or the mindfulness exercises I did before studying to manage my anxiety. It can't measure how hard I fought to stay engaged despite everything going on in my life, or how much this course meant to someone with limited resources trying to build a better future. The data sees a student who barely attended but passed—it doesn't see the whole person behind those numbers.
